title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: The drive to colonize Mars is a testament to the human spirit of exploration, but we must not let our 'backup planet' ambitions distract us from the urgent climate crisis on Earth. [38]Turning a frozen, irradiated desert into a habitable world would take centuries of terraforming—a process that is technologically speculative at best.[/38] [37]To spend billions on a Martian colony while our own oceans are rising and forests are burning is more than an eccentricity; it is a dereliction of duty to the only home we have ever known.[/37]
texts.B: [39]Establishing a multi-planetary presence is the only way to ensure the long-term survival of the human species against existential risks, whether natural or man-made.[/39] The technology developed for Mars—water recycling, closed-loop agriculture, and high-efficiency solar—will have immediate and profound benefits for sustainability here on Earth. [37]Far from being a distraction, the 'Mars shot' is a catalyst for the very innovations we need to save our own biosphere.[/37] We are not choosing between Earth and Mars; we are choosing between stagnation and a future of infinite potential.
texts.C: I agree that the 'survival of the species' argument is compelling, but we must address the 'biological imperialism' of our presence on Mars. [40]If there is even a trace of indigenous microbial life on the Red Planet, our arrival would likely destroy it before we even realize it exists.[/40] I share the view that we should prioritize robotic exploration for now. However, [39]I disagree that the costs are unjustifiable; compared to global military spending, the budget for space exploration is a pittance.[/39] The real issue is who owns the 'new world'—will it be a global commons or a corporate fiefdom?
texts.D: [40]The psychological and physical toll of life on Mars is often overlooked in the glossy brochures of private space companies.[/40] Humans are evolved for Earth's gravity, atmosphere, and social structures; living in a pressurized tin can for years would lead to profound mental and physiological decay. [38]While I share the skepticism regarding the 'terraforming' timeline, I believe the focus should be on an international moon base first.[/38] Mars is a bridge too far for a species that hasn't yet learned to live sustainably on its own doorstep. Our priority should be stewardship, not expansion.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the relationship between Mars exploration and Earth's sustainability?
questions.explanation: Expert B argues that Mars technology will help Earth's sustainability. Expert A argues that focusing on Mars is a 'distraction' and a 'dereliction of duty' to our current climate crisis.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the feasibility or timeline of terraforming Mars?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls terraforming 'technologically speculative at best.' Expert D also 'shares the skepticism regarding the terraforming timeline.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to B regarding the necessity of space investment for human progress?
questions.explanation: Expert B sees it as choosing a 'future of infinite potential.' Expert C agrees the budget is a 'pittance' compared to other spending and finds the survival argument 'compelling.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the primary risk or 'real issue' of Mars missions?
questions.explanation: Expert C focuses on 'biological imperialism' and ownership models. Expert D focuses on the 'psychological and physical toll' and the 'physiological decay' of humans themselves.
